config.title: Raising the income of the poorest matters for inequality
config.abstract: Most measures of inequality fail to account for overall economic development. The Prosperity Gap tracks both economic growth and its distribution. Although there has been improvement in the Prosperity Gap worldwide, many countries – particularly in Sub-Saharan Africa – have regressed in recent decades.

content.text: Economic development is commonly evaluated by examining the growth in GDP per capita. However, this metric says nothing about how such progress is shared among the wider population. Hence, when we care about the poorest people in the world, there is a need to look at how prosperity is shared, and how inequality develops.

The World Bank tracks the number of economies with high inequality, defined as economies with a Gini index over 40. In 2024, 44 of 169 countries had high inequality. A significant progress compared to the 72 economies with high inequality in 1995.

content.title: The number of economies with high inequality has almost halved in the past three decades
content.subtitle: High inequality are economies with Gini index > 40 and low inequality are those with Gini index < 30
content.source: World Bank Group’s Poverty and Inequality Platform (version March 2026). https://pip.worldbank.org/key-inequality-indicators
content.visdescription: The figure reports the number of countries with a Gini index over 40 from 1995 to 2024.
content.keyFactLabel: The number of economies with high inequality today, which is almost half the number in 1995.

scenes.text: The number of economies with high inequality (i.e., Gini index > 40) has decreased substantially since the 1990s. In 1995, there were 72 economies with high inequality, by 2024, there were 44 such economies – or close to half that in the 1990s.
At the same time, the number of countries with low inequality (i.e., Gini index < 30) has increased from 24 in 1995 to 34 in 2024, showing progress in reducing inequality.

content.title: Countries with high inequality are concentrated in Latin America and Sub-Saharan Africa
content.subtitle: The Gini index of each country using the most recent year with survey data
content.source: World Bank Group’s Poverty and Inequality Platform (version March 2026). https://pip.worldbank.org/key-inequality-indicators
content.visdescription: The map shows the countries with high inequality

content.text: While the number of economies with high inequality has decreased, it says little about how much or who experienced progress. In extreme cases, an improvement in the Gini index could be due to the poorest experiencing proportionally smaller income losses than the wealthy – as happened in some cases during the Covid-19 pandemic. This does not constitute progress.

Comparing the growth experienced by the poorest 40 percent of the population to the average income growth of the entire population is one way to assess progress of the poor. Progress is more equitable when the poorest 40 percent’s income growth is faster than the average income growth of the entire population.

content.title: In 3/4th of the countries between 2016 and 2021, income growth of the bottom 40 percent was faster than that of the entire population
content.subtitle: Progress is equitable when the income of the poorest 40 percent grows faster than the average income of the country
content.xaxistitle: Growth of income of the bottom 40, %
content.visdescription: The figure compares the growth rate of mean income and the growth rates of mean income of the bottom 40 percent of the population.

scenes.text: In the chart, each point corresponds to the income growth of a particular country. The 45-degree line, also known as the line of equality, reflects equal growth rates for the incomes of the bottom 40 percent and the entire population. Data points located to the right of this line indicate that income among the bottom 40 percent increased more rapidly than the national average income.

Between 2016 and 2021, the income of the bottom 40 percent in [emphasis: Bolivia ]increased by more than 4 percent, whereas the average income grew by less than 1 percent. In contrast, during the same period, the bottom 40 percent in [emphasis: Jamaica ]experienced a decline in their income while the country's average income rose by over 2 percent. Although both nations saw overall economic growth, income gains in Bolivia were more evenly distributed, benefiting lower-income groups, while Jamaica observed a relative decrease in prosperity among its poorer population segments.

Measuring income growth within the bottom 40 percent offers a practical means to assess shared prosperity. Nevertheless, this metric does not capture all aspects of inequality and presents certain limitations.

For example, even though we can compare income growth of the bottom 40 percent across Latin American and Caribbean countries, it is hard to see which country improved the most from where it started. Mexico's bottom 40 percent has less income growth, but the average person in Mexico is still richer than the average person in Peru. This difference isn't shown in the bottom 40 measure.

content.text: GDP per capita, the Gini index, or the bottom 40 measures alone do not provide a comprehensive assessment of global prosperity and how it is shared. This requires examining income levels, the pace of income growth, and how this growth is distributed across the population. Accurately assessing both overall prosperity and its distribution is essential for identifying vulnerable population groups, which is fundamental for designing effective, targeted interventions to foster further advancement. Relying solely on GDP per capita growth, the income growth of the bottom 40 percent, or any inequality measures does not adequately facilitate such analysis.
The Prosperity Gap: combining income growth and inequality

The World Bank has introduced the [emphasis: Global Prosperity Gap] as a key vision indicator  for monitoring inclusive progress. This measure integrates both average income growth and advancements in equity—reflecting reductions in inequality—into a single metric. Furthermore, the indicator assigns greatest emphasis to income growth among the lowest-income segments of the population.

The Prosperity Gap is defined as [emphasis: the factor by which incomes must be multiplied to reach a global prosperity standard]. At present, this standard is set at $28 per person per day, which is approximately equal to the average income of a person living in an economy that is at the threshold of high-income status. [reference: kraay2023]

content.title: Global prosperity standard
content.units: per person per day in 2021 PPP$
content.label: Approximately equivalent to the average income of a typical individual living in a country graduating from middle to high-income status.

content.text: Consider an individual earning $14 a day and another earning $7 a day. To reach the $28 a day standard, the first individual must [emphasis: double ]their income while the second must increase their income [emphasis: fourfold]. Consequently, the Prosperity Gap is 2 for the first individual and 4 for the second. Notably, each time income doubles, the Prosperity Gap halves.

The Prosperity Gap can be calculated for an individual, as shown in the example above, or for any defined population group. Accordingly, the Prosperity Gap of the population can be separated to reflect the contribution of each individual. This approach lets us analyze data in more detail and custom tailor policy suggestions, unlike the Gini index or bottom 40 percent income growth, which only look at groups as a whole.

content.title: The Prosperity Gap tracks both income level[emphasis:  ]and how it is distributed
content.subtitle: The Prosperity Gaps for all individuals in Peru and Colombia

scenes.text: Consider the income distributions of two countries, Peru and Colombia. Let’s suppose that there are 100 people in each country ranked from poorest on the left to richest on the right. The height of each bar represents the income of each individual in 2025.
The Prosperity Gap measures the income multiple necessary to reach the global prosperity standard of $28 per day. The smaller the level of income, the larger the increase necessary to close the gap.
For example, the income of the 10th poorest person in Peru is $4 and of that in Colombia is $3.5.

The Peruvian with an income of $4 needs to increase their income [emphasis: 7 times] to reach the prosperity standard, while the Colombian with an income of $3.5 needs to increase their income [emphasis: 8 times] to reach the same standard.
Individuals with lower incomes require a substantially greater increase in income to reach the prosperity standard.

For instance, the [emphasis: 10][emphasis: th][emphasis:  poorest ]Colombian would need to increase their income [emphasis: 8 times] compared to just over [emphasis: 5 times] for those the [emphasis: 20][emphasis: th][emphasis:  poorest].
It is important to note that individuals with lower income in Colombia face greater challenges in achieving the prosperity standard compared to similar individuals in Peru.

A country’s Prosperity Gap is the average gap of all individuals in that country. The Prosperity Gap is higher in Colombia (4.5) than in Peru (3.6), reflecting the larger gaps among the poor in Colombia.

While the Gini index shows that Colombia is more unequal than Peru (Colombia’s Gini is 54, while Peru’s is 41), it does not tell us anything about the country’s income level. People in Colombia earn more on average ($20 per day) than people in Peru ($14 per day). The Prosperity Gap, on the other hand, is a measure that combines both income levels and the effects of inequality.

content.text: Now let’s look at the trends in the Prosperity Gap of countries and the world.
Tracking the Prosperity Gap over time

content.title: From country gaps to regional and global gaps
content.subtitle: Aggregating country gaps weighted by the country’s population gives the regional and global Prosperity Gaps

scenes.text: The Prosperity Gap for Colombia in 2025 is 4.5 and that for Peru is 3.6.

The Prosperity Gaps can be estimated for each country. Each square here represents a country’s Prosperity Gap in 2025, colored by their geographic region. The size of the square represents the population of each economy.

Each region’s Prosperity Gap is estimated as the average across the countries of that region accounting for respective population sizes. On average, an individual living in Sub-Saharan Africa would have to increase their income close to [emphasis: 12] times to reach the global prosperity standard. In comparison, an individual living in East Asia would “only” have to increase their income [emphasis: 2.7] times to reach the same prosperity standard.

Similarly, the Global Prosperity Gap combines the Prosperity Gaps of all countries, again accounting for population sizes. In 2025, a typical person in the world would have to increase their income [emphasis: 4.6] times to reach the prosperity standard of $28 per day.

content.title: Global progress, regional differences
content.subtitle: While Prosperity Gap has decreased globally over the last four decades, regional differences are substantial
content.keyFactLabel: The number of years Sub-Saharan Africa is behind the world in reducing the Prosperity Gap.

scenes.text: The Global Prosperity Gap has more than halved from incomes needing to increase more than [emphasis: 11-fold] in [emphasis: 1981 ]to income needing to increase less than [emphasis: 5-fold] in [emphasis: 2025]. This significant progress reflects the poorer countries and people catching up to the richer countries and people.

All regions have made significant progress since 1981. The Prosperity Gap has plummeted in East Asia and Pacific from having the largest Prosperity Gap to having the third lowest. However, the most recent decade has seen stagnation in the Prosperity Gap of the Middle East, North Africa, Afghanistan and Pakistan and Sub-Saharan Africa. Note that Sub-Saharan Africa today has a Prosperity Gap that is close to what the global average was in 1981. By this metric, the region is 40-years behind global progress.

The Prosperity Gaps in Colombia and Peru have experienced similar developments in recent decades - including periods of increasing Prosperity Gaps most notably during the Covid-19 pandemic. In Nigeria, the level today is similar to that of 1981 - although this hides an initial sharp increase followed by substantial improvements in the late 1990s and 2000s. Worryingly, however, in Nigeria and other countries in Sub-Saharan Africa, such as Mozambique and Zambia, the improvement in the Prosperity Gap has stalled in the last two decades, and even worsened more recently.

content.text: The change in the Prosperity Gap can be due to [emphasis: change in average income] or [emphasis: change in inequality ]or[emphasis:  both]. To see this, consider first what happens to the Prosperity Gap if every Colombian’s income increased by 10 percent. This change leaves inequality unchanged and all the change in the Prosperity Gap is due to the change in average income.

content.title: Raise everyone’s income by 10 %
content.textParagraph: A 10 percent increase in everyone’s income will [emphasis: increase ]the average income by $2 from $20 to $22. As a result, the Prosperity Gap [emphasis: decreases] by 10 percent from 4.5 to 4.1.

content.text: Next, consider what happens if every Colombian earns an extra $10. This would [emphasis: increase the average income] and [emphasis: reduce inequalit]y in the country. Inequality decreases because an additional $10 dollar matters relatively more for a poorer person than a richer person.

content.title: Raise everyone’s income by $10
content.textParagraph: If everyone earns an extra $10, the average income increases from $20 to $30. The Prosperity Gap falls from 4.5 to 1.3. A third of the change in the Prosperity Gap (over 120 percent reduction) is driven by an increase in average income and the rest is driven by the reduction in inequality.

content.text: For any country, see how much their current Prosperity Gap changes due to the change in income. As above, there are two ways to change income: (a) the dollar amount (or absolute change), and (b) a percent amount (or relative change). Choose any country and adjust the sliders below.

references.kraay2023: Kraay, Aart; Lakner, Christoph; Özler, Berk; Decerf, Benoit; Jolliffe, Dean; Sterck, Olivier; Yonzan, Nishant. 2023. A New Distribution Sensitive Index for Measuring Welfare, Poverty, and Inequality. Policy Research Working Paper; 10470. © World Bank. http://hdl.handle.net/10986/39913.
